body.value: ○佐嘉藩
佐嘉藩 (さがはん)。古い表記。現代では「佐賀」が標準。

Saga domain (written 佐嘉, the kyūjitai form). Modern Japanese uses 佐賀. The dataset's domain_jp uses 佐嘉藩 for vol2 entries and the canonical name 佐賀藩 in the analytic layer. Saga has NO vol1 entry in HI — it appears only in vol2, in Tables 6, 7, 8, 9 (a gap documented in memory reference_saga_HI_gap).
○佐嘉藩
高 貳拾壹萬千五百拾九石九升九合
高 (たか) 二十一万一千五百十九石余

Omotedaka-equivalent = 211,519 koku. The formal-title kokudaka of Saga (the Nabeshima domain's bakufu-registered rating). The actual productive land was much larger via new-field development — see the 新田高 column to the left.
高
新田高 (外二) 五拾壹萬三千五百八拾石貳斗壹升六合貳勺
新田高 (しんでんだか・外二) 五十一万三千五百八拾石余

New-field high = 513,580.22 koku. Saga had massive new-field development across the Edo period — the Nabeshima domain led on intensive paddy reclamation in the Saga Plain. The combined formal + new-field total approaches 725k koku, dramatically larger than the 357k formal omotedaka most secondary sources cite.
新田高
貮口合 平均 七拾貳萬四千七百三拾九石三斗壹升五合貳勺
貮口合 平均 (にこうごうへいきん) 七十二万四千七百三十九石余 — 五ヶ年平均

Two-mouth combined, 5-year average = 724,739.3152 koku. This is Saga's unusual phrasing for what is essentially godaka (= sōdaka in Saga's case): the combined high of formal + new-field, averaged over 5 years. The 貮口合 (ni-kou-gō, 'two-mouth combined') phrasing is distinctive to Saga's HI entry and reflects the domain's split-territory administrative pattern.
貮口合 / 平均
正租 米 貳拾壹萬三千四百七十八石貳斗七升貳合貳勺
正租 (せいそ) 米 二十一万三千四百七十八石余

Main tax rice (seiso) = 213,478.27 koku. About 30% of the 貮口合 724,739 total — high effective tax rate, characteristic of Saga's tightly-administered agricultural economy under Nabeshima fiscal discipline.
正租
